response_raw:
{
   "mode": "human",
   "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
   "opinion": "I think the best way to handle fake stuff online is by being aware of it without getting too worked up. It's like spotting a liar in person; you don't just believe everything someone says, especially not when they're trying to sell something or stir things up.\n\nFor example, I used to see those viral videos claiming miracle weight loss from some fad diet. Now that I'm older and more critical, I question the source right away if it looks too good to be true. Social media is just like word-of-mouth back in high school – you gotta check their credibility.\n\nSince my parents didn't always have great examples growing up (dad barely made it through middle school without a diploma), I learned early on that people lie sometimes for no reason at all. Even worse, some accounts are obviously bots spewing nonsense 24/7.\n\nI'd recommend unfollowing any account that doesn't hold you accountable or provide real value.",
   "reasoning_summary": "Based on my creative but low conscientiousness personality combined with high extraversion and neuroticism scores (meaning I'm impulsive yet prone to anxiety), fake content resonates most when it feels personal. As a native-born American in Los Angeles who spends time weekly on social media, exposure comes from diverse sources like news feeds versus niche communities.\n\nI started by identifying which types of fake claims trigger my skepticism – exaggerated diet results or overly simplistic solutions. My low openness score means I don't seek out complex explanations automatically; instead, I rely on the emotional cues in posts (like inflammatory language) that match my high neuroticism.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think the best way to handle fake stuff online is by being aware of it without getting too worked up. It's like spotting a liar in person; you don't just believe everything someone says, especially not when they're trying to sell something or stir things up.

For example, I used to see those viral videos claiming miracle weight loss from some fad diet. Now that I'm older and more critical, I question the source right away if it looks too good to be true. Social media is just like word-of-mouth back in high school – you gotta check their credibility.

Since my parents didn't always have great examples growing up (dad barely made it through middle school without a diploma), I learned early on that people lie sometimes for no reason at all. Even worse, some accounts are obviously bots spewing nonsense 24/7.

I'd recommend unfollowing any account that doesn't hold you accountable or provide real value.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Based on my creative but low conscientiousness personality combined with high extraversion and neuroticism scores (meaning I'm impulsive yet prone to anxiety), fake content resonates most when it feels personal. As a native-born American in Los Angeles who spends time weekly on social media, exposure comes from diverse sources like news feeds versus niche communities.

I started by identifying which types of fake claims trigger my skepticism – exaggerated diet results or overly simplistic solutions. My low openness score means I don't seek out complex explanations automatically; instead, I rely on the emotional cues in posts (like inflammatory language) that match my high neuroticism.
